coredata.dc:title: A surface-induced assembly strategy to fabricate flexible carbon nanofiber/coal-based carbon dots films as free-standing anodes for high-performance sodium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Hui
Qu, Xiaoxiao
Xing, Baolin
Zeng, Huihui
Kang, Weiwei
Cheng, Song
Xing, Yaowen
He, Jingfeng
Zhang, Chuanxiang
coredata.dc:description: Flexible electrodes with superior stability, high performance, and low cost have drawn numerous considerations in energy storage devices. Here, we developed a facile and robust strategy to prepare coal-based carbon dots (i.e., CDs) with high yields derived from lignite by microwave-assisted mild oxidation and further fabricate a carbon nanofiber film decorated with CDs (i.e., CNF@CDs) by electrospinning water-soluble CDs with polyacrylonitrile (i.e., PAN) through thermal treatment and carbonization and applied as flexible electrodes for sodium-ion batteries. The as-prepared CNF@CDs as binder-free anode presents a reversible capacity of 223 mAh g−1 at 20 mA g−1 and obtains a high capacity retention of 96% over 400 cycles. The outstanding electrochemical properties were attributed to the unique structure of CNF@CDs with the optimal ratio of carbon nanofiber and coal-based carbon dots, in which carbon fiber with the 3D framework facilitates the speed of ion diffusion and electron transport as well as the surface-induced assembly of CDs on the surface of the PAN fibers brings in strong structure stability and electrode integrity. The current study supplies a novel and effective approach to constructing flexible electrodes with superior stability and high performances from low-cost materials, which has potential application prospects in flexible energy storage devices.

head.ce:title: A surface-induced assembly strategy to fabricate flexible carbon nanofiber/coal-based carbon dots films as free-standing anodes for high-performance sodium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible electrodes with superior stability, high performance, and low cost have drawn numerous considerations in energy storage devices. Here, we developed a facile and robust strategy to prepare coal-based carbon dots (i.e., CDs) with high yields derived from lignite by microwave-assisted mild oxidation and further fabricate a carbon nanofiber film decorated with CDs (i.e., CNF@CDs) by electrospinning water-soluble CDs with polyacrylonitrile (i.e., PAN) through thermal treatment and carbonization and applied as flexible electrodes for sodium-ion batteries. The as-prepared CNF@CDs as binder-free anode presents a reversible capacity of 223 mAh g−1 at 20 mA g−1 and obtains a high capacity retention of 96% over 400 cycles. The outstanding electrochemical properties were attributed to the unique structure of CNF@CDs with the optimal ratio of carbon nanofiber and coal-based carbon dots, in which carbon fiber with the 3D framework facilitates the speed of ion diffusion and electron transport as well as the surface-induced assembly of CDs on the surface of the PAN fibers brings in strong structure stability and electrode integrity. The current study supplies a novel and effective approach to constructing flexible electrodes with superior stability and high performances from low-cost materials, which has potential application prospects in flexible energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Renewable energy sources have extensively dominated the modernized market in energy storage, such as portable electronics and electric vehicles [1–3]. Among them, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs), as a potential substitute for lithium-ion batteries, have gained much attention due to the low cost and rich abundance of sodium [4–7]. However, the large radius of the Na-ion (i.e., RNa+ = 1.07 Å) makes it difficult to apply commercial lithium-ion battery anode (i.e., graphite) to SIB anode because of the limited interlayer spacing of graphite [8–10]. Therefore, there is tremendous significance in seeking appropriate anodes for SIBs with reasonable structure and excellent electrochemical performances.

One-dimensional (1D) carbon materials have been numerously researched because of their unique performances along the geometry and abundant active sites, including carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon nanowires (CNWs), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which verified rapid reaction kinetics and boosted the capacity of energy storage [11,12]. Electrospinning, a cost-effective and versatile method, has been employed to synthesize 1D carbon fibers with specified composition for energy storage applications. Furthermore, the carbon fibers obtained by electrospinning were readily interwoven into films, which are suitable ingredients for flexible and self-supporting electrodes in energy storage systems [13–15]. For example, CuO quantum dots decorated polyacrylonitrile (i.e., PAN)-based carbon nanofibrous films were prepared through a feasible electrospinning route and applied as flexible anodes for SIBs with advanced performances [16]. Lignin-blended PAN-based carbon nanofibrous webs were reported as self-supporting membranes for SIB anodes with enhanced properties by an easy electrospinning approach [17]. FeS-incorporated sulfurized PAN-based carbon fiber matrix was synthesized via simple electrospinning as a binder-free SIB anode [18]. These publications demonstrate that PAN has been extensively researched as a fascinating precursor to synthesizing self-supporting and flexible electrodes with targeted components due to its easy spinnability and high mechanical strength. Nevertheless, based on diverse environmental problems and the severe shortage of resources to explore low-cost and designed electrode materials with high deformation stress and excellent electrochemical properties is still a challenge for their application in energy storage systems. Desirably, carbon dots may be a promising alternative integrated with functional materials to develop flexible membranes applied in multi-fields due to their good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups, and small size [19–23]. It’s worth mentioning that the carbon dots with high yield derived from low-cost and abundant coal and its derivatives through mild oxidation have been reported in our previous publications [24–29].

In this work, a flexible carbon nanofibers/coal-based carbon dots composite film has been fabricated as a self-supporting SIB anode by involving the electrospinning of PAN and coal-based CDs and thermal treatment, in which the carbon dots extracted from low-cost lignite with high yields and excellent water solubility, revealing the great potential to adjust the flexibility of the PAN-based carbon nanofiber. The as-prepared free-standing CNF@CDs pellicles exhibits an outstanding reversible capacity of 223 mAh/g at 20 mA g−1 and excellent cycling stability after 400 cycles (the retention of 96 %). Impressively, the introduced carbon dots improve the flexibility of the as-prepared carbon nanofiber/coal-based carbon dots membrane and bring in strong integrity of electrodes; moreover, the as-prepared carbon nanofiber/coal-based carbon dots composite possesses interconnected structures with 3D networks, which accelerates the speed of ion diffusion and shortens the distance of electron transport, indicating boosted reaction kinetics and enhanced electrochemical performances. The current work provides an effective route to integrate carbon dots-based self-standing anodes for potential applications in flexible energy storage devices.

Lignite (from Inner Mongolia) without pretreatment was ground and filtered with a size of 200 mesh. Dialysis bags with a molecular weight cutoff of 100 Da were purchased from the Union Carbide Corporation.

The preparation process of CDs is placed in the Supplementary Material, which also was schematized in Fig. 1 a. Poly (acrylonitrile) (PAN, 0.8 g, Mv = 15000) was mixed with DMF (8 mL) at 60 °C for 12 h to produce the uniform PAN solution. CDs were dissolved into 4 mL DMF and ultrasonicated for 30 min and then added into PAN solution under mechanical stir. The stock was transferred to a syringe with a flow rate of 0.6 mL h−1 to collect the nanowires. Firstly, the precursors were dried for 10 h at 80 °C; consequently, the stock was stabilized for 2 h at 270 °C; eventually, the prepared precursor was calcined for 2 h at 1100 °C under Ar atmosphere. According to the content of CDs (x = 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 g), the as-prepared carbon nanofiber@carbon dots sample was named CNF@CDs-x (CNF@CDs-0.2, CNF@CDs-0.4, and CNF@CDs-0.6, respectively). In comparison, the sample without the introduction of CDs was called by CNF. The characterizations and electrochemical performances of the samples are provided in the Supplementary Material.

The photophysical properties of the as-prepared coal-based carbon dots were measured by UV − vis and PL spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 1b, the UV − vis absorption spectrum of CDs presents two characteristic peaks at 338 and 226 nm, the former representing n → π* transition of the surface functional group C = O bond and the latter donating the π → π* transition of the carbon core [30,31]. Furthermore, Fig. 1c shows the PL emission spectra excited by CDs at 398 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence (the right photograph of Fig. 1a). Moreover, the as-prepared CDs exhibit an excitation-independent PL behavior (Fig. 1d). In addition, the microstructure parameter was detected by TEM technology and the result was displayed in Fig. 1e and the inset of Fig. 1e (HR-TEM image), where the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm is observed, corresponds to the (100) crystal face of graphite, demonstrating the as-prepared CDs possesses graphite-like crystalline carbon structure [32,33]. The diameter distribution of the as-prepared CDs is located at 12–26 nm and the average diameter is ∼ 19.4 nm as presented in Fig. 1f. According to the XRD pattern in Fig. 1g, the CDs display a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.1°, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of graphite, in which the broad peak is due to the small size and little amorphous nature of the CDs [34–37]. FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1h show the functional groups of CDs. The C − O and C = C group were found at 1113 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1; the band at 1713 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of C = O and − CH, respectively; the peak at 3439 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibrations of − OH [38,39]. The surface chemical composition was further analyzed by the XPS technique and the resulting data were exhibited in Fig. 1i-k. CDs mainly contain three elements with the content of ∼ 75.22 % of C, 21.91 % of O, and 2.87 % of N in Fig. 1i. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 1j verified the existence of the sp2 hybridized orbital of carbon, which corresponds to C = C/C − C (284.7 eV), C − O (286.2 eV), and C = O (288.6 eV) [40,41]. As presented in Fig. 1k, the O1s spectrum was divided into three peaks at ∼ 533.9, ∼533.0, and ∼ 531.5 eV, which was indexed to C − OH, C − C = O, and C = O, respectively [42]. Consequently, these results demonstrated various polar functional groups, including C − O, C = C, C = O, −CH, and − OH, which were following the FT-IR results, guaranteeing the superior solubility of the CDs and revealing the great potential as a functional addition to adjusting diverse material via combining with other precursors in multi-field application [43,44].

The preparation process of the as-prepared carbon nanofiber@coal-based carbon dots is presented in Fig. 2 . Briefly, CNF@CDs were obtained by electrospinning technology with polymer-PAN as a spinning carrier, CDs as a loading addition, and DMF as the solvent via combining thermal treatment and carbonization. Furthermore, carbonized fiber film possesses high flexibility and can withstand a certain amount of twisting without breaking. The digital photographs of as-spun fiber films, preoxidized fiber films, and carbonized fiber films were presented in Fig. S1 (a-c) and the related description was given in Supplementary Material. The CNF@CDs-0.2 film is flexible and foldable, bent and re-expanded many times, then can be restored to its original shape without breaking. As presented in Fig. S1 (d-f), a complete electrode material can be obtained after cutting, and the electrode material is not easily broken. Fig. S2 displays the digital image of the CNF prepared by PAN spinning. As can be seen from all angles, the untreated raw filament before the pre-oxidization and carbonization presented some softness and white color similar to the toilet paper, as shown in Fig. S2a; while after pre-oxidation and carbonization in Fig. S2b, the filament has lost its flexibility and becomes extremely fragile. Impressively, the CDs added into the spinning solution might interact with PAN, in which the tertiary amino groups on PAN reacted with the hydrogens on CDs, bringing out the surface-induced assembly of CDs on the surface of the PAN fibers as shown in the dashed rectangle in the middle of Fig. 2, and thus facilitating the preparation of binder-free and self-supporting film electrodes for sodium-ion batteries [45]; furthermore, the introduction of carbon dots fills in the vacancy on the surface of the PAN and thickens the diameters of carbon fiber. This is because abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups allow the CDs to act as cross-linking agents toward PAN molecules during carbonization, resulting in a dense, strong, and flexible carbon skeleton of the carbon nanofibers [46]. Moreover, experiments presented that the increasing of CDs would affect the spinability and flexibility of the spinning solution adversely, in which CNF@CDs-0.4 and CNF@CDs-0.6 showed a lower flexibility than that of CNF@CDs-0.2. This is because the increased contents of CDs caused the interaction between the CDs and PAN to become too weak to support the surface-induced assembly process [45].

The surface morphologies and microstructures of the CNF and CNF@CDs-0.2 composite were investigated by TEM and SEM techniques. The as-prepared pristine CNF in Fig. 3 a-b are mutually adherent and present a size distribution of 178 nm according to the statistics regarding the dimensions (inset of Fig. 3a); furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 3b, CNF@CDs-0.2 exhibited a relatively smooth surface with a more uniform diameter of about 250 nm (inset of Fig. 3b) after the introduction of carbon dots, which may contribute to ensuring structural integrity during the continuous long-term cycles. As the comparison, the SEM images and the size distributions of CNF@CDs-0.4 and CNF@CDs-0.6 were presented in Fig. S3, in which the reduced diameters of 230 nm and 228 nm further verified the optimal interaction between the CDs and PAN. This phenomenon is attributed to the weak interaction between the oxygen-bearing functional groups and the PAN molecules. Thus, the CNF@CDs-0.2 with the optimized ratio of CDs and PAN possesses the interconnected carbon nanofibers networks and may promote electronic diffusion and ionic transport [47]. The TEM images in Fig. 3c suggest the CNF@CDs present a weakly ordered turbostratic structure and the obvious boundary in Fig. 3d further confirms the successful preparation of the CNF@CDs composite [47]. The EDS mappings in Fig. 3e exhibit a uniform distribution of C, N, and O elements in CNF@CDs-0.2. Fig. 4 a and 4b show the BET results of the as-prepared CNF and CNF@CDs-x samples. According to the IUPAC classification, these isotherms belong to type IV, verifying the mesoporous existed in the as-prepared four samples; moreover, the rapid increase of adsorption capacity under high relative pressure indicates the existence of macroporous structures in the CNF and CNF@CDs-x samples. The macropore is conducive to the rapid infiltration of electrolytes and effectively reduces the diffusion distance of Na+, thus boosting the electrochemical performances. Table 1 lists the pore structure parameters of the CNF@CDs with different content of CDs. The specific surface areas of CNF, CNF@CDs-0.2, CNF@CDs-0.4, and CNF@CDs-0.6 calculated by BET are 10.90, 5.06, 4.54, and 4.040 m2/g, respectively. The pore volume decreased in the order of CNF (0.041 cm3 g−1) > CNF@CDs-0.2 (0.028 cm3 g−1) > CNF@CDs-0.4 (0.024 cm3 g−1) > CNF@CDs-0.6 (0.023 cm3 g−1). These results exhibit that the pore volume and specific surface area of samples become smaller after the addition of CDs, which may be caused by the carbon dots filling the vacancy on the surface of the PAN and bringing in the thickened diameters of carbon fiber as shown in Fig. 3a, 3b, S3a, and S3b. Such a low surface area was conducive to the suppression of SEI formation and the reduction of irreversible capacity. The introduction of CDs on the CNF increases the flexibility of the as-prepared CNF@CDs samples, indicating the structural integrity and excellent reversibility of electrodes during the sodium storage process. According to the BJH method, the pore size of all samples in Fig. 4b is distributed in the range of 1 ∼ 200 nm with numerous mesopores and macropores [48–50]. Fig. 4c shows the XRD patterns, in which the CNF presents two broad peaks at 43.9° and 24.7°, which is attributed to the (100) and (002) diffraction modes, indicating the disordered carbon. Furthermore, along with the increase of CDs content, the two peaks become gradually faint, demonstrating the as-prepared samples present amorphous structures, which is consistent with HR-TEM results. The Raman spectra (Fig. 4d) reveal two bands at 1590 and 1347 cm−1, corresponding to G-band and D-band, respectively. The intensity ratio (R) of the D and G band (R = I D/I G) is an index to assess the disorder degree; the R values of CNF, CNF@CDs-0.2, CNF@CDs-0.4, and CNF@CDs-0.6 are 0.90, 0.88, 0.86 and 0.82, respectively. With the CDs content increases, the lattice defects become smaller and the arrangement becomes more regular. It’s worth pointing out that the large interlayer spacing and weakly ordered turbostratic structure may be conducive to the reversible storage of Na+ [51]. The surface functional groups of CNF@CDs-x were evaluated by XPS and the C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s were observed, as shown in Fig. 4e. The lower oxygen content of CNF@CDs observed in C 1 s mainly comes from the C = O and C − O bond (Fig. 4f). Due to the high carbonization temperature (1100 ℃), CNF@CDs only exists C = O bond in Fig. S4. Table 2 lists a small amounts of element N, which may come from DMF and PAN. Consequently, the existence of element N can enhance the electronic conductivity and electrochemical reactivity, thereby suggesting a high reversible capacity during the charge/discharge cycles [52,53].

The CNF@CDs films were directly employed as self-supporting sodium-ion battery anodes to estimate the electrochemical properties in half-cells with a counter electrode of Na metal foils. Fig. 5 exhibits CV curves from 0.01 V to 2.8 V at 0.1 mV s−1 of the CNF and CNF@CDs-x anodes. Interestingly, the profile of the initial cycle presents a different shape compared with that of the 2nd and 3rd cycles, where a wide peak around ∼ 0.9 V vanishes during the following sodiation reaction, which was ascribed to the formation of solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) layer and the decomposition of organic electrolyte [54]. Besides, subsequent curves show excellent repeatability without obvious deformation, implying an outstanding reversibility of the CNF@CDs-x. This is because numerous defect sites were captured Na+ in the first cycle, and thus in the next cycles the reversibility was markedly improved. Fig. 6 depicts the GCD profiles at 20 mA g−1 of CNF and CNF@CDs-x in 0.01–2.8 V (vs. Na+/Na), including voltage plateau and tilt regions, below ∼ 0.1 V and above ∼ 0.1 V, respectively, demonstrating two types of sodium ion storage behavior in CNF films, which corresponds to CV analysis. Specifically, the plateau region corresponds to the sharp peak value, which represents the process of sodium ion embedding inside carbon microcrystals and filling in the internal pores; the inclined region is caused by the adsorption of sodium ions by the defect site and is related to the reversible adsorption/desorption of Na+ at the edge/defect of the carbon layer in the CNF films [55]. The Coulombic efficiency (CE) of CNF, CNF@CDs-0.2, CNF@CDs-0.4, and CNF@CDs-0.6 in Table 3 were 23.5 %, 68.2 %, 68.2 %, and 72.0 %, respectively. The improved CE may be attributed to the high crystallinity, relatively ordered structure, fewer defects, and relatively low specific surface area of CNF@CDs-x. The low surface area was conducive to the suppression of SEI formation and the reduction of irreversible capacity. Besides, high graphitization and fewer defects also reduce the irreversible reactions and enhance the ICE during the first cycle [56–58].

Fig. 7 a shows the rate performance of CNF@CDs-x anodes, the average specific capacities of CNF@CDs-0.2 are 223, 219, 196, 105, 81, 73, 68, and 56 mAh g−1 at 20, 50, 100, 300, 500, 1000, and 2000 mA g−1, respectively. Remarkably, the CNF@CDs-0.2 anode retains a high capacity of 220 mAh g−1 when returned to 20 mA g−1, and the CNF@CDs-0.4 and CNF@CDs-0.6 electrodes also maintained 181 and 184 mAh g−1, respectively, which verifies an exceptional rate property and further implying the structure integrity without obvious degradation under high-rate test during the charge/discharge processes. This is due to the 3D interconnected structure constituted with 1D carbon nanofibers can greatly accelerate the reaction kinetics. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) without cycling tests was used to estimate sodium storage performances of CNF and CNF@CDs-x. Nyquist plots in Fig. 7b and S5 include tilted straight lines in the low-frequency region and semicircles in the mid-high-frequency region, which is attributed to solid-state diffusion and charge transfer resistance of Na+ in the electrode material [59,60]. The inset of Fig. 7b profiled the equivalent circuit model. These results demonstrate that the CNF@CDs-0.2 anode presents a faster transfer rate of sodium ions and electrical conductivity than those of CNF, CNF@CDs-0.4 and CNF@CDs-0.6. Therefore, the CDs in the carbon nanofiber frameworks can form an interconnected conductive network and notably improve the charge-transfer kinetics and Na+ migration rate in the deeply branched micropores.

The cycling performance is an essential indicator to evaluate the potential in practical application. The long-term properties after 400 cycles were conducted at 100 mA g−1 and presented in Fig. 7c. Expectedly, the reversible capacity of CNF@CDs-0.2 anode was maintained compared with that of other samples. Specifically, after a few cycles of stabilization, the capacity of CNF@CDs-0.2 reaches 196.1 mAh/g and after 400 cycles, a capacity retention of 96 % is retained [61], while after 400 cycles CNF@CDs-0.4 displays the capacity of 73.5 mAh/g and CNF@CDs-0.6 exhibits a fast capacity loss (3.2 mA h g−1).

The CDs play a significant role in enhancing the pseudocapacitive activity and electrolyte wettability. The capacity contribution was determined on the various scan rates of the CV curves in Fig. 8 a to assess the electrochemical behaviors, in which the reduction peak at ∼ 0.9 V becomes more obvious gradually with the scan rate increasing because of the polarization and depletion of the electrons [62–65]. The capacity contribution of the carbon nanofiber electrode is evaluated by the value of b in an equation, i = aνb (i, ν, and a is the peak current, scan rate, and constant, respectively) [65–67]. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the cathode peaks and anode peaks in CV curves were selected to fit the calculation, and the obtained b values were 0.52 and 0.95 (between 0.5 and 1), respectively, indicating the sodium storage of carbon fiber was conducted by both capacitance characteristic and diffusion behavior [68,69]. Furthermore, the contribution rate of sodium storage capacity is determined by a formula, i(V) = k1ν + k2ν1/2 (k2ν1/2 and k1ν are the response current values contributed by capacitance and diffusion process, respectively), and the outcomes are presented in Fig. 8c and Fig. S6. It is worth mentioning that at a low scan rate, the sodium storage capacity of carbon nanofibers is occupied by diffusion behavior; with the scan rate increases, the capacitance characteristic gradually dominated, increasing from 46.41 % to 65.52 % for CNF@CDs-0.2. The capacitance contribution ratios of CNF@CDs-0.2 are higher than that of CNF@CDs-0.4 and CNF@CDs-0.6 at each scanning speed, further confirming the optimal interaction of CNF@CDs-0.2, thus bringing in enhanced rate performance and cycling stability during the charge/discharge reaction [62,70]. SEM image in Fig. 8d and 8e presents the CNF@CDs-0.2 film still maintains integrity with a super long structure without obvious collapse, demonstrating excellent structural stability, which guarantees superior electrochemical properties. The 3D carbon nanofiber frameworks in Fig. 8f could provide abundant space for electron transfer and ion diffusion, boosting superior sodium storage performance. The introduction of coal-based carbon dots into pure PAN to prepare flexible anodes for sodium-ion batteries through the electrospinning method possesses electrochemical performances, which are ascribed to the following advantages: (i) the self-supporting film directly constructs the integrated electrode without the addition of binders reduces the “dead mass” and capacity loss. (ii) the doped N element enhances the electronic conductivity and electrochemical reactivity and thus brings in remarkable storage capacities. (iii) 1D carbon frames built to a 3D conductive network as reservoirs provide abundant open space for sodium-ion storage as well as shorten the diffusion path for Na+ storage, and thus result in excellent reversible capacities.

In summary, we developed a facile and robust route to fabricate flexible and free-standing carbon nanofiber/carbon dots composite films as binder-free anodes for sodium-ion batteries. The carbon dots prepared from low-cost lignite with high yields and excellent water solubility revealed the great potential to adjust the flexibility of the carbon nanofiber. The as-prepared self-supporting CNF@CDs films as anode for SIBs exhibited an excellent reversible capacity of 223 mAh g−1 at 20 mA g−1 and outstanding cycling stability. Impressively, the oxygen-containing groups on carbon dots interact with tertiary amino groups on PAN, resulting in the improved flexibility and strong integrity of electrodes during the charging/discharging processes. Moreover, the as-prepared carbon nanofiber/coal-based carbon dots composite possesses interconnected structures with 3D networks, which accelerates the speed of ion diffusion and shortens the distance of electron transport, indicating boosted reaction kinetics and enhanced electrochemical performances. The current study supplies a novel method to construct carbon dots-based high-performance anodes for potential application in flexible energy storage devices.
